citation: Walia, B., Owora, A., Kmush, B., Ehrlich, J., & Sanders, S. (2024). Head impact, concussion, and salary in the NFL: Is there a compensating wage-risk premium? American Behavioral Scientist. https://doi.org/10.1177/00027642241260774
abstract: This study examined whether NFL players in position groups with higher estimated cumulative head impact or concussion risk receive a compensating wage premium in their next contract, controlling for productivity (Wins Above Average), experience, and contract year. Using 1,162 fully observed player contracts and 290 non-rookie contracts from the 2006–2017 NFL seasons, frequency-weighted fixed-effects linear regressions were specified. Results showed players are compensated for productivity and experience, but position groups with higher head impact or concussion risk receive a significant salary dispremium rather than a premium, consistent with an augmented compensating salary-differential theory in which both firm and employee share risk.
tldr: Contrary to standard labor economic theory predicting a wage premium for hazardous work, NFL players at higher-risk head-impact positions actually earn less on their next contracts. Teams appear to discount salaries to offset projected future healthcare liabilities and roster depth costs associated with high-concussion positions. Net effect is an estimated $2.54 million per-season dispremium for head impact risk exposure.
methods: Fixed-effects linear regression with frequency weighting by contract length. Dependent variable: next-contract average annual salary. Independent variables: player productivity (WAA from Pro Football Focus), years of NFL experience, experience squared, and three alternative measures of head impact risk (cumulative Head Impact Risk Factor, Concussions Per 100 Games Played, Concussions Per 1,000 Athletic Events) by position cluster. Twelve model specifications total, including six using lagged productivity for the non-rookie sub-sample (n=290). Data span 2006–2017 NFL seasons.
findings: Across all 12 model specifications, higher head impact risk and higher concussion rate were associated with significantly lower next-contract salaries (dispremium). At mean concussion rate and salary, the estimated salary decrease was approximately $5.25 million/season for concussion risk; the net overall head-impact dispremium was approximately $2.54 million/season. Players are compensated for sub-concussive head impacts (a premium), but penalized more strongly for concussive head impact risk, resulting in a net dispremium. Productivity (WAA) and experience are positively and significantly associated with salary.
limitations: Study limited to skill positions (QB, WR, RB/TE) where WAA data were available. Does not cover all NFL positions. Head impact risk measures are position-cluster estimates, not individual player measurements. Salary cap dynamics and collective bargaining effects may confound results. Does not account for positional depth chart differences or strategic roster construction beyond salary modeling.
practicalImplications: NFL teams appear to be financially responding to head impact risk by reducing salaries at high-risk positions rather than paying a risk premium. This has implications for player contract negotiations, union advocacy, and long-term healthcare liability planning. Organizations should consider the financial and ethical dimensions of how head injury risk is factored into compensation structures.
athleteDev: Findings highlight that positions with higher cumulative head impact exposure (e.g., running backs, wide receivers) face market-level salary penalties, which may influence athlete position decisions and career longevity considerations. Development staff should be aware that players at high-impact positions face compounded risks—both neurological and financial.
rtp: The study's evidence that concussive head impact risk reduces team demand for individual players has indirect RTP implications: teams may have financial incentives to limit playing time for high-concussion-rate positions, which could informally influence RTP timelines. Staff should advocate for RTP decisions based purely on clinical criteria rather than financial considerations.

citation: Boone, R. T., Zambrotta, N. S., Manocchio, A. M., & Bowman, J. K. (2025). Head in the game: The impact of cognitive abilities on performance of National Football League quarterbacks. Frontiers in Psychology, 16, 1540498. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2025.1540498
abstract: This study examined whether cognitive abilities measured by the Athletic Intelligence Quotient (AIQ)—Visual Spatial Processing, Reaction Time, Decision Making, and Learning Efficiency—predicted NFL quarterback performance beyond what is explained by draft pick. AIQ scores from 42 NFL quarterbacks collected at the Combine (2014–2020) were matched to performance metrics including Career Approximate Value (CAV), Quarterback Rating (QBR), passing yards, rushing yards, Big Time Throw rate, Turnover Worthy Plays, and Throwing Accuracy. Hierarchical regressions controlling for draft pick showed that Reaction Time predicted CAV, QBR, rushing yards, and games started; Visual Spatial Processing predicted passing yards; and an interaction between Visual Spatial Processing and Decision Making predicted Turnover Worthy Plays and Throwing Accuracy.
tldr: Cognitive performance measures—especially Reaction Time and Visual Spatial Processing—predict multiple NFL quarterback performance statistics above and beyond what draft pick alone predicts. Quarterbacks with faster reaction times have higher career value and passer ratings; better visual spatial processors gain more passing yards and throw more accurately. These findings support incorporating cognitive assessment in QB evaluation and development.
methods: Participants: 42 NFL quarterbacks who declared for the draft from 2014–2020 and played at least 3 games. Instrument: Athletic Intelligence Quotient (AIQ), administered at the NFL Combine via tablet software (AIM, LLC). Performance metrics sourced from Pro Football Reference (CAV, QBR, passing/rushing yards) and Pro Football Focus (Big Time Throw rate, Turnover Worthy Plays, Throwing Accuracy). Hierarchical multiple regression with draft pick entered at Step 1 and AIQ subscales entered at Step 2; trimmed models retained only significant AIQ predictors. Mixed model analysis used for PFF statistics (year nested within player).
findings: Reaction Time predicted games started per year (+6% variance beyond draft pick), CAV (+13%), QBR (+8%), and rushing yards per game (+9%). Visual Spatial Processing predicted passing yards per game (+7%). Interaction of Visual Spatial Processing × Decision Making significantly predicted Turnover Worthy Plays and Throwing Accuracy. Big Time Throw rate was not predicted by any AIQ factor. Learning Efficiency showed no significant relationships with any performance metric. Overall, AIQ cognitive factors added predictive power to 6 of 7 performance metrics examined.
limitations: Small sample size (n=42) limits statistical power and generalizability. Only quarterbacks who played at least 3 games were included, creating a survivor bias. Model excludes team-level factors such as offensive line quality and coaching scheme. Games started per year used as a proxy for ability may be confounded by organizational investment in high draft picks. Psychological facets such as arousal regulation and resilience are not captured by the AIQ.
practicalImplications: Cognitive assessment tools like the AIQ offer meaningful predictive value for QB performance and should be considered alongside physical metrics in scouting and player development. Reaction Time in particular is a broadly useful cognitive marker. Coaching staff might focus on drill designs that develop rapid perceptual decision-making and visual field scanning to improve QB performance.
athleteDev: For QB development programs, training environments that emphasize rapid decision-making under pressure and visual scanning of defensive formations could translate to improved on-field performance. The AIQ framework provides a structured way to identify cognitive development targets for young quarterbacks entering developmental pipelines.
rtp: Cognitive performance measures may have utility in RTP assessment for quarterbacks recovering from concussion or other neurological events. The AIQ subscales—especially Reaction Time and Visual Spatial Processing—could serve as sensitive markers of cognitive recovery, given their demonstrated relationships to on-field QB performance.

citation: Park, G., Gabler, L. F., Bailey, A. M., Dau, N. Z., Sherwood, C., Crandall, J. R., & Arbogast, K. B. (2021). Head shape analysis of National Football League players. Proceedings of the Institution of Mechanical Engineers, Part P: Journal of Sports Engineering and Technology. https://doi.org/10.1177/17543371211020614
abstract: Current football helmet fitting guidelines rely solely on head circumference, which may not optimize fit or protection for every player. This study used statistical shape analysis (SSA) and principal component analysis (PCA) on 3D light scans of 87 NFL players to identify key features of head shape variability. Two principal components explained 87% of variance: (1) head height and length in the sagittal plane and (2) prominent occiput with oval transverse cross-section versus prominent forehead with round cross-section. Four measurements—circumference, length, breadth, and height—were defined and quantified. NFL players had significantly larger circumference, length, and breadth than U.S. Army soldiers in the ANSUR II database.
tldr: NFL player heads are larger and more variable in shape than standard male populations, and current helmet fitting based solely on circumference misses important shape dimensions. Two principal components—head height/length and oval vs. round transverse profile—explain 87% of shape variance. Adding breadth and height measurements to fitting protocols could substantially improve helmet fit and safety for professional football players.
methods: 3D structured light scans (Artec Eva, 0.5 mm resolution) of 129 NFL players from three teams prior to the 2019–2020 season; 87 scans included after excluding those with hair obstructing scan quality. Five anatomical landmarks defined a head coordinate system; pseudo-landmarks (N=1,278) were mapped from each scalp in spherical coordinates. Correlation-based PCA performed in MATLAB on normalized pseudo-landmark data. Four head shape measurements (circumference, length, breadth, height) compared between NFL sample and ANSUR II database (n=4,082 male U.S. Army soldiers) using Student t-tests.
findings: First PC (74.8% of variance): tall vs. short head—primarily related to sagittal height and length. Second PC (12.3%): prominent occiput with oval transverse shape vs. prominent forehead with round shape. Together, two PCs explain 87% of head shape variability. NFL players had significantly larger circumference (592.3 vs. 574.4 mm), length (212.2 vs. 199.5 mm), and breadth (168.2 vs. 154.3 mm) than ANSUR II soldiers (all p<0.05). Height was also significantly larger (152.8 vs. 131.1 mm). Correlations among shape parameters were similar between groups.
limitations: Sample of approximately 5% of current NFL players limits generalizability. Insufficient data to analyze head shape differences by playing position. Players with certain hairstyles (dreadlocks, ponytails) were excluded, which may introduce sampling bias. Effect of hair on helmet fit was not addressed. Standard headforms used in laboratory helmet testing (NOCSAE, Hybrid III) do not represent NFL player head shapes.
practicalImplications: Helmet manufacturers should consider multi-dimensional head shape parameters beyond circumference when designing and sizing helmets for NFL players. Incorporating length, breadth, and height measurements into fitting protocols could improve protection. Current laboratory headforms underrepresent NFL player head size and shape, which may affect the validity of helmet performance testing for this population.
athleteDev: Proper helmet fit is fundamental to player safety. Equipment staff should ensure that helmet fitting accounts for head shape dimensions beyond circumference—particularly for players with oval vs. round head profiles—as poor fit may compromise both comfort and concussion mitigation. Position-specific helmet designs may eventually be warranted.
rtp: Helmet fit has been linked to concussion risk; helmets that fit poorly due to shape mismatch may increase impact transmission. Understanding an athlete's individual head shape could inform post-injury helmet fitting reviews as part of a comprehensive RTP protocol to reduce re-injury risk.

citation: Shanley, E., Thigpen, C., Kissenberth, M., Gilliland, R. G., Thorpe, J., Nance, D., Register-Mihalik, J. K., & Tokish, J. (2019). Heads Up Football training decreases concussion rates in high school football players. Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine, 00, 1–7. https://doi.org/10.1097/JSM.0000000000000711
abstract: This prospective cohort study compared sport-related concussion (SRC) rates and recovery in 2,514 high school football players during the 2015–2016 season: 1,818 players from 14 schools receiving US Football Heads Up (HU) training versus 696 players from 10 control schools with no HU training (NHU). HU-trained coaches instructed proper blocking and tackling technique emphasizing heads-up contact. Results showed HU players had a significantly lower overall SRC rate (4.1 vs. 6.0 per 100 players; RR=1.5) and practice-specific SRC rate (RR=1.9). HU players also returned to full participation 27% faster (18.2 vs. 24.8 days).
tldr: The US Football Heads Up program reduced sport-related concussion rates by 33% and shortened recovery time by 27% in high school football players over one season. Practice-specific concussion rates were nearly twice as high in untrained players, suggesting technique training is especially valuable during practice. This is the first prospective efficacy study of the HU program on SRC incidence in scholastic football.
methods: Prospective cohort study. 14 Greenville County high schools (n=1,818 players) received US Football HU certification (player safety coach training) before the 2015–2016 season; 10 comparison schools (n=696 players) continued standard coaching. Random fidelity audits (minimum 3 per school) confirmed ≥80% compliance with HU technique instruction and performance. Athletic trainers and sports medicine physicians documented all SRCs prospectively. Cumulative incidence, incidence rates (per 100 players and per 100,000 athlete exposures for practices), and rate ratios with 95% CIs were calculated. Time loss compared by one-way ANOVA.
findings: 117 total SRCs observed. HU rate: 4.1 SRCs/100 players vs. NHU rate: 6.0 SRCs/100 players (RR=1.5; 95% CI 1.1–5.4). Practice SRC specific incidence rate: 2.6 vs. 4.9 per 100,000 athlete exposures (RR=1.9; 95% CI 1.1–3.2). Game SRC rate trended higher in NHU (RR=1.67; 95% CI 1.0–2.8). Time to full return: HU=18.2 days (95% CI 15.8–20.6) vs. NHU=24.8 days (95% CI 19.9–29.7), a 27% reduction. All HU schools maintained ≥80% fidelity throughout the season.
limitations: Assignment to training groups was not randomized; groups had unequal sample sizes. HU players were significantly heavier than NHU players. Game-level exposure by individual player was not available, limiting specific game incidence rate calculations. Position-specific data were not collected. Study conducted over a single season in one geographic region, limiting generalizability. Results require replication in larger, more controlled designs.
practicalImplications: Implementing the US Football Heads Up training program—particularly proper blocking and tackling technique instruction—can reduce concussion rates and recovery time in high school football. At least one coach should complete HU certification and serve as a player safety coach to ensure consistent technique instruction throughout all levels of practice. Athletic trainers and sports medicine physicians should support program fidelity monitoring.
athleteDev: Technique training focused on heads-up blocking and tackling—emphasizing breakdown position, controlled foot movement, heads-up hit position, and hip-drive shooting—reduces head contact frequency and severity in developing athletes. Integrating these principles from the earliest practice days through the full season is critical for long-term athlete safety and development.
rtp: HU-trained athletes returned to full participation approximately one week faster than untrained athletes following concussion, under the same standardized RTP protocol. This suggests that reduced concussion severity—potentially due to improved technique reducing impact magnitude—may facilitate faster symptom resolution and progression through return-to-play stages.

citation: Walton, S. R., Carneiro, K. A., Smith-Ryan, A. E., Stoner, L., Kerr, Z. Y., Brett, B. L., Chandran, A., DeFreese, J. D., Mannix, R., Lempke, L. B., Echemendia, R. J., McCrea, M. A., Guskiewicz, K. M., & Meehan, W. P. III. (2025). Health-promoting behaviors and their associations with factors related to well-being among former National Football League players: An NFL-LONG study. Journal of Athletic Training, 60(2), 185–197. https://doi.org/10.4085/1062-6050-0537.23
abstract: This cross-sectional study from the NFL-LONG cohort examined associations between health-promoting behaviors (exercise frequency, diet quality, sleep duration, sleep disturbance) and well-being outcomes (Meaning and Purpose, Self-Efficacy, Ability to Participate in Social Roles and Activities) in 1,784 former NFL players (mean age 52.3 years). Multivariable linear regression models were fit for the full sample and for six age subgroups. Less sleep disturbance was associated with better outcomes on all three well-being measures across nearly all age groups. More frequent moderate-to-vigorous exercise was associated with better Meaning and Purpose and Social Role Participation. Diet quality and resistance training were not broadly associated with well-being outcomes.
tldr: In 1,784 former NFL players, lower sleep disturbance and more frequent moderate-to-vigorous exercise were the most consistent predictors of better subjective well-being across the lifespan. These two modifiable behaviors represent key clinical targets for improving overall health and well-being in former professional football players as they age.
methods: Cross-sectional survey (online via Qualtrics or paper-based) from the NFL-LONG study, conducted January 2019–April 2020. Former NFL players (n=1,784; mean age 52.3±16.3 years) completed the survey. Health-promoting behaviors assessed: moderate-to-vigorous exercise frequency, resistance training frequency, other wellness activities, REAPS diet quality score, nightly sleep duration, and PROMIS-29 Sleep Disturbance T-score. Well-being outcomes: PROMIS Meaning and Purpose SF-4a, PROMIS Adult Self-Efficacy SF-4a, PROMIS Ability to Participate in Social Roles and Activities 4-item scale. Multivariable linear regression models adjusted for demographic, behavioral, and functional covariates; separate models fit for full sample and six age subgroups (<30, 30–39, 40–49, 50–59, 60–69, 70+ years). Multiple imputation used for missing data.
findings: Sleep disturbance was negatively associated with Meaning and Purpose (β=−0.196), Self-Efficacy (β=−0.185), and Social Role Participation (β=−0.137) in the full sample and across almost all age subgroups. More frequent moderate-to-vigorous exercise was associated with better Meaning and Purpose (β=0.068) and Social Role Participation (β=0.151). Resistance training, diet quality, sleep duration, and other wellness activities were not broadly significant in the full-group models. Age-group specific patterns emerged, with exercise associations particularly pronounced among players aged 40–69 years. Diet quality was associated with better Self-Efficacy only among players <30 and 30–39 years.
limitations: Cross-sectional design limits causal inference. Survey response frequency was relatively low (1,784/15,025 = ~11.9%), limiting generalizability. Self-reported behaviors may be under- or over-estimated. Exercise session duration was not captured, so meeting aerobic guidelines cannot be confirmed. Data collection overlapped with the onset of COVID-19 in spring 2020, which may have affected exercise and sleep behaviors. Mild cognitive impairment and dementia—more prevalent in this population—were not directly measured and could affect self-reported outcomes.
practicalImplications: Former NFL players represent a population at elevated risk for chronic health conditions and well-being decline as they age. Clinicians treating former players should prioritize sleep quality assessment and intervention alongside exercise prescription. Encouraging moderate-to-vigorous aerobic exercise—particularly in the 40–69 age range—appears to be an especially high-impact strategy for improving multiple dimensions of well-being.
athleteDev: For active players approaching the end of their careers, establishing sustainable exercise habits and healthy sleep routines during their playing years may support long-term well-being post-retirement. Organizations should integrate lifestyle education and behavior-change support into career transition programs.
rtp: Sleep disturbance is both a common post-concussion symptom and a primary predictor of poor well-being in former players. This study reinforces the importance of targeting sleep quality as part of comprehensive concussion management and long-term athlete health monitoring, particularly as players age and accumulate career exposure.
